findings.body: retainForReprocessing() keeps retained blocks/envelopes after startProcessing() clears successfulDownloads but discards the current attempt, so later peer-attributable failures can carry peers: [] and avoid scoring the original sender.
findings.note: retainForReprocessing now carries Attempt; verified at 01fe150bed and approved in review 4741092726

events.reason: new related GitHub review comment in same file/line region

findings.body: Peer-attributable EXECUTION_ENGINE_INVALID/PAYLOAD_ERROR_EXECUTION_ENGINE_INVALID attempts are stored in executionErrorAttempts but excluded from getFailedPeers(), so the same bad peer can be selected immediately again instead of being excluded from retry.
findings.note: getFailedPeers now includes peer-attributable executionErrorAttempts; verified at 01fe150bed and approved in review 4741092726

events.reason: new related GitHub review comment in same file/line region

findings.body:
hashing blocks + envlopes are cheap enough thanks to `cachePermanentRootStruct` option
the downside is we don't include signatures, haven't seen an issue of same block with different signatures for range sync on any networks yet
findings.note: hashBlocks includes block/envelope signatures in attempt id

events.reason: new related GitHub review comment in same file/line region

findings.body:
**<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Handle first-batch parent faults as unrecoverable here**

When this is the first queued batch in a sync chain and its first block fails with `PARENT_UNKNOWN`/`PARENT_PAYLOAD_UNKNOWN`, `prevBatch` is `undefined` but this still returns `PreviousBatch`. `processBatch()` then calls `retainForReprocessing()` and invalidates no earlier batch, so the same bad downloaded batch is retried without re-downloading or inc
findings.note: First-batch parent fault no longer stalls; no-prev-batch path classifies as ThisBatch

events.reason: new related GitHub review comment in same file/line region

findings.body:
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Keep execution-engine failures out of peer scoring**

Including `executionErrorAttempts` here can downscore peers after transient local EL failures: `Batch.processingError()` records `EXECUTION_ENGINE_ERROR` separately via `onExecutionEngineError()`, and teardown intentionally maps `MAX_EXECUTION_ENGINE_ERROR_ATTEMPTS` to no peer report, but a later successful retry with a different hash will now report the e
findings.note: Local EL errors remain blameless; only peerAttributable attempts are reported/scored

events.reason: new related GitHub review comment in same file/line region

findings.body:
Confirmed — this is real, and I think it's blocking. Traced end to end:

`classifyProcessingFault` returns `PreviousBatch` whenever `firstBlockSlot === batch.startSlot`, without ever checking `prevBatch`. For the first batch of a sync chain `prevBatch` is `undefined`, and then:

1. `batch.retainForReprocessing()` → `Processing` → `AwaitingProcessing`, blocks retained, **no attempt recorded** — by design, its docstring says so explicitly.
2. `invalidatePreviousBatches()` requires `pendingBatch.st
findings.note: First-batch parent fault no longer stalls; no-prev-batch path classifies as ThisBatch

findings.body:
Confirmed, though I'd rank it below the P1. The strongest evidence is that the PR is internally inconsistent on this point: `shouldReportPeerOnBatchError` maps `MAX_EXECUTION_ENGINE_ERROR_ATTEMPTS → null` (*"If peer should not be downscored, returns null"*) directly alongside `MAX_PROCESSING_ATTEMPTS → LowToleranceError`. So the teardown path already asserts EL errors are not the peer's fault, while `advanceChain` now scores exactly those attempts one screen up.

`onExecutionEngineError` only fi
findings.note: Local EL errors remain blameless; only peerAttributable attempts are reported/scored

findings.body:
The cheapness argument holds — `blockRootHex` is already cached, so this is strictly cheaper than the old `SignedBeaconBlock.hashTreeRoot` path.

On signatures, I'd frame the risk differently than "same block with different signatures". BLS signing is deterministic, so an honest peer serving the same block message always produces the same signature — you're right that this doesn't arise naturally, and no network would have shown it.

The case that does bite is adversarial: a peer serves the **co
findings.note: hashBlocks includes block/envelope signatures in attempt id

findings.body: 🔴 Peer-attributable EL `INVALID` attempts need to be excluded from immediate retry too. `routeProcessingFailure()` now marks `EXECUTION_ENGINE_INVALID` / `PAYLOAD_ERROR_EXECUTION_ENGINE_INVALID` attempts with `peerAttributable: true`, but they are stored in `executionErrorAttempts`; this method only returns failed downloads and `failedProcessingAttempts`. Since `peerBalancer` uses this set to skip peers for the next attempt, the same peer that just served definitively invalid execution data can
findings.note: Inline retry-exclusion finding addressed; verified at 01fe150bed and approved in review 4741092726

findings.body: 🔴 `retainForReprocessing()` should preserve the current attempt's peer provenance. `startProcessing()` has already cleared `successfulDownloads`, and this transition keeps the retained blocks/envelopes while dropping `this.state.attempt`. On the later reprocess, `startProcessing()` builds a fresh attempt from an empty successful-peer map, so if the retained data then fails a peer-attributable check such as block/envelope signature validation, the failure can carry `peers: []` and avoid scoring t
findings.note: Inline retained-provenance finding addressed; verified at 01fe150bed and approved in review 4741092726
